content.text: Every Angular application is TypeScript, and TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript. Understanding how JavaScript stores values and controls their visibility is not optional background knowledge — it is the foundation every Angular concept builds on. The way signals, Observables, and dependency injection work all depends on lexical scope and variable lifecycle.

content: JavaScript has three ways to declare a variable —
, and
— each with different scoping rules, hoisting behaviour, and mutability constraints.

content: JavaScript uses
(also called static scope): the scope of a variable is determined by where it is written in the source code, not by how the code is called at runtime.
Variables declared outside any function or block are global. In browsers, they attach to
; in Node.js, to
. In Angular, you almost never intentionally create global variables — the module system and dependency injection replace that need.
Variables declared with
or
(and
) inside a function are local to that function. Parameters are also function-scoped.
Any pair of curly braces creates a block.
and
are confined to that block.
ES modules (TypeScript files) create a module scope. Top-level declarations in a
file are NOT global — they belong to that module's scope and must be explicitly exported to be accessible elsewhere. This is the default in Angular.
When a variable is referenced, JavaScript looks it up starting from the innermost scope and walks outward until it finds a binding or reaches global scope and throws a
. This chain of scopes is fixed at the time the code is written.

content.text: Hoisting is JavaScript's compile-phase behaviour of moving declarations to the top of their scope. The specifics differ by keyword:

content: A closure is a function that
its outer scope even after the outer function has returned. Closures are not a feature you enable — every function in JavaScript is a closure.

A closure referencing a component that is destroyed keeps the component in memory. Always unsubscribe RxJS subscriptions and clear intervals in
Destructuring Assignment with let and const

content: Destructuring lets you unpack values from arrays and objects directly into
or
bindings in a single statement. It is used pervasively in Angular code — component inputs, route params, RxJS results, and function parameters all benefit from it.

content.text: Shadowing occurs when an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer scope variable. The inner binding hides (shadows) the outer one within that scope.
Shadowing is sometimes intentional (narrowing a parameter name in a callback) but usually indicates a naming problem. TypeScript's no-shadow ESLint rule flags unintended shadowing:

content: The Angular CLI generates projects with ESLint pre-configured. The

content.text: rule is part of the recommended config. Keeping it on saves hours of debugging.

JavaScript variables are declared with

(when reassignment is needed), or

are block-scoped and subject to the Temporal Dead Zone. The scope chain determines variable lookup order: inner scope first, outward to module/global. Closures — every function remembers the scope where it was created — power Angular's event handlers, RxJS operators, and DI factories. In TypeScript,
adds compile-time immutability for object properties. Default to
and only reach for
when you know the binding will change.
